text: This is a video to describe the technical aspects in acute cholecystitis in a young lady with a history of pain in abdomen since three days and multiple such episodes in the past with severe pain in the right upper abdomen and leukocytosis.
The initial inspection reveals numerous omental adhesions surrounding the gallbladder area.
The surgeon begins the procedure by performing adhesiolysis to carefully separate the omental adhesions observed during the initial inspection.
The surgeon uses the left hand to provide counter traction while employing a hook instrument to manipulate tissue, following the technique of 'hook, look, and cook' for precise dissection.
The surgeon uses a hook to grasp the tissue, visually inspects the area, and then applies cautery to the tissue.
The pneumoperitoneum aids in dissecting the tissue planes, allowing the surgeon to access the correct anatomical plane while minimizing collateral damage.
The surgeon continues adhesiolysis in the right upper abdomen, retracting the colon downward and laterally to release the lateral attachments of the omentum.
These kind of adhesions signify that this patient had multiple episodes of pain in the past.
The surgeon continues adhesiolysis along the edge of the liver, using surgical instruments to carefully separate adhesions from the liver tissue.
And the principle of surgery here is traction, counter traction and action.
The surgeon uses the left hand to provide counter traction, assisting in the dissection process by stabilizing the tissue and enhancing visibility of the surgical planes.
The surgeon continues adesolysis near the gallbladder, carefully separating adhesions to enable retraction of the gallbladder fundus.
The surgeon observes fluid-laden tissue, indicating an episode of acute cholecystitis, as the dissection continues along the liver edge.
The surgeon completes the dissection of omental adhesions on the lateral aspects, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon addresses the medial aspect of the surgical field, methodically bringing down adhesions using traction and counter traction techniques.

After completing the adhesiolysis, the surgeon uses a hook to perform dissection, followed by the use of a suction cannula to further dissect and clear the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a dissecting tool to open up tissue planes in the area of Calot's triangle, avoiding energy devices to clearly delineate the anatomy.
The surgeon uses a hook instrument to assist in dissecting tissue, visibly separating anatomical planes and structures.
The surgeon resumes monopolar dissection using blend current mode in the area of Calot's triangle, focusing on precise tissue separation.
The surgeon uses monopolar current to carefully dissect layer by layer, ensuring precise separation of tissue planes.
The surgeon continues the edizolysis in the area of the Calot's triangle, using a suction cannula to assist in the dissection process, visibly separating tissue planes.
The surgeon identifies the vessel originating from the cystic artery that supplies the cystic duct, focusing on the anatomical structures within the dissected area.
The surgeon uses monopolar electrosurgery to coagulate the vessel, creating a visible space between the cystic artery and the cystic duct.
The surgeon uses the left-hand instrument in a splaying technique to carefully dissect between the cystic artery and cystic duct, separating these vital structures.
After completing the dissection, the surgeon identifies the critical view of safety, ensuring clear visualization of the cystic duct, cystic artery, and the liver bed.
The surgeon completes the posterior dissection and reflects at least 2.5 centimeters of the gallbladder upward to achieve the critical view of safety.
The surgeon creates a visible window between the cystic artery and cystic duct, highlighting the space formed during dissection.
The surgeon clips and cuts the cystic artery, completing its separation from the cystic duct.
The surgeon uses a laparoscopic technique to milk out the cystic duct, applying pressure to expel its contents during the cholecystectomy procedure.
The surgeon identifies a small stone at the level of the cystic duct and uses a milking technique to move the stone upwards.
Two Hem-o-lok clips are visibly positioned on the white cystic duct, securing it during the cholecystectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses a titanium large clip to secure the cystic duct after milking it upwards, ensuring the duct is properly clipped.
The surgeon carefully cuts the cystic duct evenly to prevent any residual tissue at the distal end, which could lead to slippage of the stump.
The surgeon reflects the gallbladder using the flagging technique, manipulating the gallbladder on either side to improve exposure of the surgical field.
The surgeon flags the gallbladder on both the right and left sides, employing the 'hook, look, and cook' technique to manipulate and assess the gallbladder during the procedure.
It's important to do it in this sequence so as to avoid any damage because of the direct or indirect application of the current.
The surgeon completes the final dissection around the fundus of the gallbladder, ensuring the area is fully separated from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon safely removes the gallbladder from the fossa without any spillage, placing the specimen into a retrieval bag for extraction.
After placing the gallbladder specimen in a retrieval bag, the surgeon performs a gentle saline lavage in the surgical area to visually inspect for any bleeding spots.
The surgeon addresses low pressure, low volume bleeding by applying a small piece of Surgicel to the affected area, aiming to control the bleeding effectively.
It's always better to avoid any kind of gladiating currents to be applied on the omentum.
The surgeon places a drain in the surgical area using an NG tube, following the removal of an acutely inflamed gallbladder.

The surgeon widens the epigastric port to facilitate the removal of the gallbladder specimen, which is then extracted through the port. A small metadine gauze is used to clean the port area after removal.
